folio.label: 164v
folio.l: may nat do ,

And secoundly , he that is Irous & wrotú , he ne may
nat wel deme ,

And he that may nat wel deme , may nat wel
cou~seille ,

The thridde is this , that he æ is Irous & wrotú , as
seitú Senek , ne may nat speke , but blameful thynges ,

and
witú hise vicious wordes , he steretú oæere folk , to angre & to
Ire

¢ And eke Sire , ©e muste dryue coueitise out of ©oure her-
te ,
for the Apostle seitú , æ coueitise is the rote of alle harmes ,

And trostetú wel , æ a coueitous man , ne kan nat deme , ne th-
enke , but oonly to fulfille the ende of his coueitise ,

& certes æ
ne may neuäe ben acomplised , For euäe the more habundaunce
æ he hatú of richesse , the more he desiretú

¢ And sire ©e muste also
dryue out of ©oure herte , hastifnesse , for cätes

©e ne moun nat
deme for the beste , a sodeyn thou©t , æ falletú in ©oure herte ,
But ©e muste a vyse ©ow on it ful ofte ,

for as ©e herde here
biforn~ , the co~e ¸uerbe is this , that he æ sone demetú , sone
repentetú ,
Sire , ©e ne be nat alwey , in like disposiciou~ ,

for
cätes som thyng , æ somtyme semetú to ©ow , æ it is good for
to doo , A nother tyme , it semetú to ©ow the contrie ,

whan ©e
han taken cou~seil vn to ©oure self , And han demed by good delibe-
raciou~ , swicú thyng as ©ow semetú best

¢ Tha~ne rede I ©ow , æ
©e kepe it secree , 171Cross in dry point in the right margin.ODR

by wreye nat ©oure cou~seil to no ¹sone , but if
so be , æ ©e wenen sikerly , æ thurgú ©oure bywreiynge , ©oure co~-
diciou~ shal ben to ©ow more ¸fitable ,

For Iúc Syrak seitú , Ney-
ther to thy foo , ne to thy frend , diskeure nat thi secree , ne æi
folie ,

for they woln ©eue ©ow audience & lokyng & supportacion
in thy päsence , & skorne the in thyn absence

¢ Another clerk
seitú , æ skarsly shalt æè fynden any ¹sone , æ may kepe thy
cou~seil secrely

¢ The book seitú, whil æ æè kepest thy counseil
in thyn herte , æè kepest it in thy pésou~ ,

And whan æè bewreyest
thy cou~seil , to any wigút , he holdetú the in his snare ,

& æäfore
©ow is bettre , to hide ©oure cou~seil in ©oure herte , than preye hi~
to whom ©e han by wreyed ©oure cou~seil , æ he wol kepe it clos
& stille

¢ For Seneca seitú , If so be , æ æè ne maist nat , thyn
owen cou~seil hide , how darstow preyen , any oæä wigút , thyn
cou~seil secrely to kepe

¢ But nathelees , if æè wene sikerly , æ thy
biwreiyng of æi cou~seil to a ¹sone , wol make thy condiciou~ , to
stonden in the bettre pli©t , tha~ne shalt æè telle hi~ thy cou~seile
in this wyse

¢ First æè shalt make no semblau~t , wheiæä the were
leuere pees or werre , or this , or that , ne shewe hi~ nat thy wil ,
and thyn entente ,

for troste wel , æ co~ely theise cou~seillours ben
flatereres ,
namely the cou~seillours of grete lordes ,

For they
enforcen he~ alwey , rather to speken plesau~te wordes , enclynyng
to the lordes lust , than wordes , that ben trewe or ¸fitable ,

&
æäfore men seyn , æ æe riche man hatú selde good cou~seil , but if he
